author: @ericlotze7724
text:
Minor Bit on DME.  I an unsure if it can be used in a Spark Ignition Engine (hard to find papers on this, at least for 100% DME, not a LPG Blend).  HOWEVER it works VERY WELL in Compression Ignition (Diesel) Engines.

Similar to how propane (or as it is often called for vehicle use, Autogas) and CNG are nice for Spark Ignition engines in terms of how well it vaporizes and such that carries over.

Also *unlike* CNG which some diesels are modified to use, it stores more densely and easily. (Although “Adsorbed Natural Gas” may give it a run for it’s money, you should try and replicate it as well)

Most Interesting However *it cannot form Diesel Particulate Matter* or “soot”.  So as long as you aren’t burning oil, in theory you can do a pile of exhaust gas recirculation and maybe a *little* urea so can be compliant with emissions way easier.  No need for a Diesel Particulate Filter, or at least no need to “regenerate” or clean it!

author: @michaelsohocki1573
text:
Dear Rob, after watching this video I became interested in biopropane production, and discovered (in an article released this month that can be found in a link on Wiki) that a private company called Orbex, headquartered in the UK, is building a spaceport in Sutherland, Scotland--and their chief source of fuel for getting the rockets into space is biopropane. I've emailed them asking how this became financially and technologically viable on a large scale and in what must surely be one of the most energy-intensive activities we DO as a species. 

I hope to see this technology incorporated into the foundations of daily life, such as the municipal power grid (here in San Antonio, an embarrassing amount of it still comes from burning coal).

Like you've said so many times, in so many ways: all you've got to do is do it.

author: @matthewgardenstheglobeboec7153
text:
What is a hydro-carbon without the carbon?? It's water.
When water is split into gases via electrolysis it produces 1 hydrogen and 2 units of oxygen.
This gas mix when burnt in a carburater produces 2800°C of heat and water again. The gases are called HHO for short (Di-hydride monoxide AKA water)
Plenty of how to videos on YouTube, why use algae? We have seas of water.

author: @dariusj33
text:
I don't think lack of dead dinosaurs is the main issue here. IMO the main issue is the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere. Dead dinosaurs had it locked under the ground, but we found it and released into the air. And now that we're running out of them, we're looking for some other CO2 sources. That's not right.

It _would_ be right if we had the means to capture and lock the produced CO2 in some way. Of course, we could have as many natural CO2 capturing and locking devices (greens: trees, grass, etc.) as we (children of the Earth) had back in the alive-dinosaurs ages. But then again, we're too focused on cutting them down instead, burning them and releasing more CO2 out.

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
​I hope you're aware of the fact, that for now(= 2:44 ) this generator is running. But as soon as the engine warms up, it'll burn it's fuel more efficient, so at first it will rev up, so the governor will throttle down. Which in this case means less air and the same amount of propane going into the engine, which is wrong air/fuel-ratio, so it dies.

Also:
If you plug in a device, the engine will have to work harder. So at first rpm will drop, then the governor will throttle up, which means more air by the same amount of fuel, so the engine dies.

This ( 2:44 ) will only work for a constant load and constant engine-temperature (and probably something else too).

If, however, you can find a way to link your propane-valve to the governor, you're in business.
I don't think such a link would have to cost a lot of money. Just some thinkering...

​@ThinkingandTinkering

Rob, several years ago, I placed a propane tank on the back of my riding lawnmower, and I cut my grass like that, for years.  You cannot throttle up or down, very much, without problems. If the mower backfires, it may produce flame at the hose, and without said device, can travel all the way to the regulator.
Good job 'mate'.

Maurice Strong was a key figure in the development of the concept of carbon credits. He was involved in the creation of the Chicago Climate Exchange (CCX), the first firm in the U.S. to trade carbon credits, and he also had links to the trading of carbon emissions credits in China
1
. His role in the green energy movement was significant, and he was known as the "godfather of the environmental movement"
4
. However, there are conflicting views on his stance regarding company profiteering and its impact on the environment. Strong's involvement in business activities, such as his links to the trading of carbon emissions credits, has raised questions about his position on company profiteering and its environmental implications
1
. Therefore, while he was instrumental in the development of the carbon credit concept, his beliefs on company profiteering in the context of environmental impact are not entirely clear from the available information.

replies.author: @audiowan
replies.text: Hello , just take a 1/2 inch copper pipe 4 inches long and pack pretty tightly with brass or bronze wool,  solder two end caps with tube nozzles....and you have a (flash back arrestor)....

replies.author: @richspillman4191
replies.text: @audiowan I worked for a welding equipment distribution warehouse back in  the mid '80's when flashback and check valves were being combined. We had a demo unit that had a clear tube section connected to a harris check valve then a clear tube section connected to a flashback arrestor with another section of clear tube. It would show that the flashback would travel past the checkvalve and then get stopped by the arrestor.  The salesman we had didn't comprehend how the test was to be performed, a mix of oxygen and acetylene would be put thru the tube and a spark lighter would be struck at the end causing the flashback. When he did the demo, he attached the tube to the acetylene regulator hose fitting and lit the end, causing billowing black sooty flames to come out of the tube. He called and asked if that was how it was supposed to work, I said "YES! IT'S WORKING SHUT IT OFF!"

author: @AbadorOlsztyn
text: How about an episode on diesel engines running on ethanol?:)

replies.author: @denisripley8699
replies.text: alcohol has a flashpoint of around 65°F, while diesel fuel has an average flashpoint of 150°F. Mixing a product with such a low flashpoint in diesel fuel is extremely dangerous

replies.author: @AbadorOlsztyn
replies.text: @denisripley8699 I was thinking more about running a diesel engine only with ethanol. I'm not sure if diesel would mix properly without any additives. Fuel is injected in the moment of highest compression so the flashpoint should be irrelevant(the temp in diesel engine is higher than of the diesel fuel)? diesel is more oily, but as a PoC I would like to see it done on a small < 1l engine with some tests(someone was doing this with an old BMW if I'm not mistaken). Aside from a lower power and probably a shorter engine lifespan it should be doable. Running a gasoline engine seems more tricky and we would need to mix it with gasoline (at least most of the tests I saw did that).

replies.author: @denisripley8699
replies.text:
​@AbadorOlsztyn Abador.. it seems pretty daunting to do what you are suggesting.  A brief search of the literature throws up this thread from IEA-AMF 

High concentration ethanol require special engines. Ethanol use in spark-ignited engines is relative easy, since the properties of ethanol resemble gasoline. FFV spark-ignition cars for high-oxygen content fuels, for example E85, are well-known and widely on market. Instead, ethanol use in the compression ignition engines is challenging. If ethanol is to be used in compression ignition engines, either the engine or the fuel has to be modified, the latter option requiring fuel additives. Ethanol differs significantly from conventional diesel fuel regarding cetane number, heating value, self-ignition temperature, vaporization characteristics, and boiling point. Cetane number of neat ethanol is less than 12, whereas today the requirement for diesel in Europe is higher than or equal to 51 (properties of ethanol). Thus neat ethanol as such will not ignite in conventional diesel engine. Also the lubricity of ethanol is unacceptable for high-pressure injection pumps in diesel engines. The low boiling point of ethanol increases the risk of cavitation, and the high conductivity the risk of corrosion (IEA-AMF Task 46 Nylund et al. 2015, Peckham 2001, McCormick 2001). In the past Detroit Diesel manufactured glow-plug equipped heavy-duty engines for using methanol or ethanol. However, many problems like engine wear, and glow-plug failures and excessive fuel consumption were encountered. Thus the production of these engines was discontinued. In Sweden another approach was selected: adding ignition improver and lubricity additive to ethanol to enable the usage of the diesel combustion process (Diesel engines for additized ethanol; Scania’s ethanol engine).

author: @gazoo9477
text: Short cycle carbon sequestration like biomass fuels are carbon neutral.  However people have trouble wrapping minds around the idea.😢

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: None are truly carbon neutral and many are not even carbon effective due to the supply and processing inputs. Often only true waste products (or near- zero processing input raw products) would qualify as neutral, and that often goes away in the wash...

author: @lordtr-d1n
text: We can easily produce methanol using solar energy. Can you try this.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Most generator manufacturers advertise that there generator makes less power on propane than on gasoline, but when you can make your own for pennies it does not matter.

replies.author: @richspillman4191
replies.text: The exhaust is less offensive with propane also.

replies.author: @551moley
replies.text: ​@richspillman4191  I had a car running on LPG, V6 Alfa I did well over 100k miles in it, I took it in for an MOT without the Catalytic converter fitted and it only just failed, the garage advised me to thrash it up A30 before  a retest, 1 hour later with the cat refitted she passed no problem!
